nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shores in the lower and mid-littoral zones; tide pool rims, shallow crevices, and sub-tidal rocky substrates to ~33 m depth

avoidWith: sea urchins (may graze heavily on calcareous algae)

imageCandidates.notes: Product image from Mosaic Macros listing for Coral Weed (Corallina officinalis)

summary: Coral Weed (Corallina officinalis) is a calcareous red alga that grows as upright, jointed, fern-like pink-purple tufts in reef aquariums. Unlike encrusting corallines that coat rock surfaces, Coral Weed builds a branching skeleton and requires stable calcium and magnesium levels to thrive.
careNotes:
Coral Weed grows as upright, jointed, fern-like tufts reaching up to about 12 cm — a form known as articulated coralline algae. This distinguishes it from encrusting corallines (genera such as Hydrolithon and Lithothamnion) that spread as flat sheets across rock surfaces. Both growth forms belong to the family Corallinaceae within the red algae division (Rhodophyta), but Coral Weed builds a jointed, pinnately-branching calcareous skeleton rather than a crust.

Calcium and magnesium are essential for the skeletal calcification that gives this alga its rigid structure. It is generally advised to maintain calcium at 400–440 ppm and magnesium at 1,250–1,400 ppm. Alkalinity around 8–12 dKH supports the carbonate availability the skeleton depends on. Phosphate and nitrate should be kept low — phosphate below 0.05 ppm and nitrate below 5 ppm — as elevated nutrient levels have been reported to inhibit coralline growth.

Lighting should be moderate to high; high-intensity reef lighting tends to support robust growth. Growth rate is slow, which makes Coral Weed manageable and well-suited to display tanks where an invasive spread would be unwelcome. Regular water changes help replenish calcium, carbonates, and trace elements for sustained growth.

IMPORTANT — DO NOT BURY IN SAND: Coral Weed will melt at the base if placed in a sand bed. It must be attached to rock using superglue, zip-ties or fishing line, or by wedging a fragment into a crevice.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is primarily by fragmentation — a detached segment of the thallus (the branching body of the alga) can settle onto rock, attach, and grow into a new colony. Sexual reproduction also occurs through an alternating cycle of spore-producing and gamete-producing generations, a life history pattern common among red algae, though this process is not commonly observed or managed in closed aquarium systems.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary trade source; confirmed species identity (Corallina officinalis), growth form, and availability as aquarium specimen. Limited husbandry details on product page.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Corallina officinalis
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Corallinaceae, Corallinales), articulated growth form, native distribution (North Atlantic, North Pacific, southern hemisphere), depth range 0–33 m, and adult size up to 120 mm. Cross-checked size and native range.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — What are Coralline Algae?
additional.notes: Secondary source confirming coralline algae water parameters: pH 8.2–8.3, Ca 415–425 ppm, Mg ~1,250 ppm, alkalinity ~10 dKH; confirmed articulated vs encrusting distinction; confirmed slow growth rate and fragmentation propagation.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
